_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Newton County, GA. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Newton County's records are generally complete from its 1821 formation. Sherman's March passed through the county but did not destroy the courthouse.

previous_courthouses.fate: Early courthouses replaced. No major fire or war damage to records despite Sherman's passage through the county in 1864.

record_losses.cause: No major courthouse fire or destruction
record_losses.description: Newton County's courthouse records survive from the county's 1821 formation with good continuity. Sherman's March passed through Covington in November 1864 but did not destroy the courthouse or its records.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in Newton County after 1821, the county records almost certainly survive — deeds, wills, and court minutes run continuously from formation.
record_losses.source: GA Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records.

deeds.repository: Newton County Clerk of Superior Court; GA Archives microfilm

wills_estates.repository: Newton County Probate Court; GA Archives

court_minutes.repository: Newton County Clerk of Superior Court

substitute_sources.description: The 1821 lottery distributed Newton County lots.
substitute_sources.repository: GA Department of Archives and History
substitute_sources.source: Cadle, Georgia Land Surveying History and Law (1991).

substitute_sources.description: Baptist and Methodist churches dominated Newton County. Church minute books supplement civil records.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual congregations; Mercer University Baptist archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for marriage, baptism, and membership documentation.

narrative_hooks: Newton County's courthouse survived Sherman's March, and {ancestor_name}'s deeds, wills, and court records are likely intact from the county's 1821 formation.

The Georgia Land Lottery records show who drew Newton County lots in 1821 — {ancestor_name}'s first claim to the red clay may be documented at the state archives even if the county records are unfamiliar.
Georgia vital registration did not begin until 1919 — the absence of a birth certificate for {ancestor_name} before that date is normal, not suspicious.
If {ancestor_name} was a sharecropper in Newton County after 1865, the county records may hold crop lien filings — the legal instruments that documented debt, not ownership.
source_references: GA Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records.
Cadle, Farris W. Georgia Land Surveying History and Law. University of Georgia Press, 1991.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: Georgia. Atlanta, 1937-1942.
